data.title: News Highlights: Top Company News of the Day - Friday at 5 AM ET
data.description:
Panasonic Holdings to Cut 10,000 Jobs Globally

The Japanese electronics maker said it would re-evaluate the numbers of organizations and personnel needed. 

 
Commerzbank Posts Higher Earnings After Commission Income Lifts Revenue

Earnings and revenue for the first quarter exceeded analyst expectations, after higher commission income offset a decline in interest income. 

 
IAG to Buy 53 Planes Despite Market Uncertainty

British Airways owner International Consolidated Airlines said it ordered the planes to bolster its long-haul fleet after earnings rose. 

 
Coinbase Reports Drop in Profit

Earlier in the day Coinbase announced a $2.9 billion cash-and-stock deal to acquire Deribit, a bitcoin options exchange. 

 
McKesson to Spin Off Medical-Surgical Company

The pharmaceutical distributor said the new company would be a differentiated medical surgical supply and solutions business. 

 
Lyft Swings to Profit Amid Increase in Ridership

San Francisco-based company said rides grew 16% year over year, to 218.4 million, ahead of analyst views for 215.1 million. 

 
CoStar Agrees to Buy Australian Advertiser Domain for $1.9 Billion

U.S. real-estate analytics provider CoStar agreed to buy property advertiser Domain in a sweetened deal. 

 
News Corp Revenue Boosted by Gains at Dow Jones, HarperCollins

Digital and print Wall Street Journal subscriptions totaled 4.34 million for the March quarter, the company says. 

 
Peloton Slashes Marketing Again in Hard Play for Profitability

Double-digit percentage cuts continue for the fourth consecutive quarter as the fitness company's marketing chief leaves. 

 
TKO Group Raises Full-Year Outlook on Recent Acquisitions, Swings to Profit

The new forecasts came as TKO Group swung to a profit and posted higher-than-expected revenue in the recent quarter.

(END) Dow Jones Newswires

May 09, 2025 05:15 ET (09:15 GMT)

data.title: 'We live modestly': My wife and I have $900K in stocks and $380K in savings and CDs. Do we have too much cash?
data.description:
By Quentin Fottrell

'My investment adviser says we have too much invested in cash instruments'

Dear Quentin,

I have $900,000 invested in the stock market and over $380,000 in CDs and savings. My spouse and I live modestly, have a paid-off car and have no debt of any kind. I am retired and my spouse is still working. We are both in our mid-70s. Also, I have a long-term-care policy. My spouse does not. I'm concerned about my ability to enjoy a comfortable retirement. My investment adviser says we have too much invested in cash instruments.

Are we on track?

Retiree

Don't miss: Suze Orman says retirees should have a 5-year 'just-in-case' fund. Is she right?

Dear Retiree,

That's a lot of cash.

How much cash you ultimately decide to have in your portfolio depends on your financial status, age and risk tolerance. For retirees like you with no mortgage payments and low costs, living off Social Security and an income from your investments, you would be safe enough with one to two years' worth of expenses in cash or CD ladders. That means you would not have to cash out any investments in a down market. That gives you options and peace of mind.

You have almost 30% of your investable assets in cash. Let's say you're getting a 4.5% return on your CDs and you're earning close to that in high-yield savings accounts. You're beating inflation, but you would be getting a lot more bang for your buck on the stock market. The S&P 500 SPX has averaged a 10% return, adjusting for prolonged bull markets, recessions, corrections and crashes, over the last century. So your adviser, in my view, is right.

And remember, money-market funds, which invest in short-term, higher-quality securities and are currently offering yields of up to 4.5%, are taxable as ordinary income - not as capital gains - and they are not risk-free. On the other hand, you can ride the peaks and valleys of the stock market and experience paper profits and losses without having to file a tax return. Keeping cash in money-market funds potentially pushes you into a higher tax bracket.

Diversification in your 70s

In your 70s, you should probably have 40% in equities, 50% in bonds and 10% in cash (rather than your 30%). You will likely remain invested in the market long enough to be able to safely withstand short- to medium-term volatility. The Cboe Volatility Index VIX, also known as the market's "fear gauge," is currently at a reading of 24, after reaching a year-to-date high of 52 in early April. Volatility is a reflection of the level of uncertainty in the market.

When talking about diversification, it's too easy to leave non-U.S. stocks off the table. As Edward Franklin Thomas, a certified financial planner in Birmingham, Ala., recently told my colleague Beth Pinsker: "Year-to-date internationals are up 11% for large cap and around 6% for small cap. Yes, a money market is probably getting around 4%, but you're leaving 2% to 7% on the table by not participating in a fully diversified portfolio."

You have almost 30% of your investable assets in cash.

Consider individual bonds, bond mutual funds or exchange-traded funds. "If you've stayed in cash because you like how your money-market fund makes regular interest payments, you may want to learn more about opportunities in bonds," according to Fidelity. "Like money-market funds, bonds pay regular interest. Unlike cash, they also give you opportunities for capital appreciation and to lock in interest payments that may be higher than what you could earn on cash in the future."

Fidelity continues: "To be sure, adding stocks and bonds to your portfolio doesn't mean cash has no role to play in your investment strategy. Over time, stocks, bonds, and cash have all taken turns as the best - and worst - performing investments. Because financial markets and the business cycle are always in motion, it's good to make sure your portfolio holds enough of all 3 of those types of assets to help you make progress toward your goals."

Navigating uncertainty

I can understand your cash-happy status. The market fell into correction territory in April before recovering, and the volatility is likely not over yet. But the long-term trajectory of the market is encouraging. The S&P 500 fell 18% in 2022, then rose 26% in 2023 and another 25% in 2024. Historical data show that it can take one month (as it did in 2023), three months (in 2015 and 2016), six months (in 2009, 2010, 2011, 2018 and 2020) or, more commonly, one year to "recorrect" by at least 10%.

Looking at history, the market, for the most part, has been resilient. That's not an opinion - it's a review of the last 100 years. It's been resilient even over the last five years, when we've lived through a worldwide pandemic and are now facing a U.S. administration that is charting a different course from that of any Republican or Democratic administration in modern times by redrawing the postwar Western alliance and introducing sweeping tariffs that have spooked Wall Street.

I'm not suggesting you do a complete overhaul of your retirement strategy. You've done pretty well to get to where you are today, and any financial adviser with a pair of eyes would see that. But there is a downside to being too cautious and too concerned about world events. We are surrounded by uncertainty, and you just need to find the best path to navigate your own worries. As Warren Buffett would tell you, that's one thing you do have control over.

Don't miss: 'I am scared to death that I'll run out of money': My wife and I are in our 50s and have $4.4 million. Can we retire early?

You can email The Moneyist with any financial and ethical questions at qfottrell@marketwatch.com, and follow Quentin Fottrell on X, the platform formerly known as Twitter.

The Moneyist regrets he cannot reply to questions individually.

More columns from Quentin Fottrell:

I'm 65, and a widow with 5 grown children. Should I split my $1.1 million investment account between them?

My husband and I have $3M and are blessed with 3 children. Are we obliged to give them each $50K for their education?

My sister made frequent withdrawals from the 'bank of Mom and Dad.' Now our family is torn apart. What can I do?

Check out The Moneyist's private Facebook group, where members help answer life's thorniest money issues. Post your questions, or weigh in on the latest Moneyist columns.

By emailing your questions to The Moneyist or posting your dilemmas on The Moneyist Facebook group, you agree to have them published anonymously on MarketWatch.

By submitting your story to Dow Jones & Co., the publisher of MarketWatch, you understand and agree that we may use your story, or versions of it, in all media and platforms, including via third parties.

.

-Quentin Fottrell

  (END) Dow Jones Newswires



  05-09-25 0516ET

data.title: European Midday Briefing: Stocks Rise on First Trade Agreement
data.description:
MARKET WRAPS

European stocks were up on Friday, buoyed by renewed optimism surrounding global trade negotiations.

Trump on Thursday agreed to a framework for a trade agreement with the U.K . but analysts question the significance.

"The markets celebrate this agreement not because it shines in every detail but because this rough framework might be sufficient to maintain the temporary suspension of countersanctions permanently ," CMC Markets said.

Pepperstone said it was less of a deal and more of an 'agreement in principle' and finally some sort of concrete progress on the trade front.

Analysts hope this agreement opens the door for futher deals.

U.S. Markets:

Stock-market futures mostly rose ahead of a weekend of trade talks with China.

Google had a bad week with shares dropping over 5% but analysts were sticking with the Alphabet stock.

Forex:

The dollar turned lower after jumping to a one-month high against a basket of currencies following the announcement of the U.S.-U.K. trade deal.

However, "the deal (or better call it non-deal) is not very substantial and does not necessarily give rise to hopes that much more substantial results can be expected in negotiations with other countries," Commerzbank said.

The euro's momentum has stalled for now but it should pick up when the Federal Reserve starts cutting interest rates later this year, RBC Capital Markets said.

RBC said enthusiasm for selling dollars has waned as trade tensions de-escalate.

However, U.S. rate cuts should help the euro, while the currency's share of global reserves will likely increase over the coming years, it said.

Bonds:

Treasury yields were expected to move moderately higher even as they face opposing forces, SEB Research said.

These included anticipated policy rate cuts versus fiscal considerations, erosion of trust in U.S. policy, unattractive valuations and rotation into European bonds.

SEB Research expected the 10-year Treasury yield to trade near 4.50% in coming months, slightly above current levels, and to increase to 4.80% in 2026.

The 10-year Bund yield was expected to remain around 2.50% in the coming months, in line with current levels, SEB Research said separately.

"Support from anticipated ECB rate cuts and rotation to eurozone government bonds are balanced by high financing needs and the ongoing Eurosystem balance sheet reduction."

Yields on government bonds rose after the BOE indicated a gradual pace of interest-rate cuts in future.

Short-dated yields rose notably, with the two-year gilt yield reaching a three-week high.

Although the BOE cut rates by 25 basis points, Danske Bank said many in the market had expected the central bank to signal an increased pace of future reductions.

Defense issuance by EU member countries was unlikely to impact government bond issuance meaningfully this year , SocGen said.

Energy:

Oil prices were on track for a weekly gain of more than 3%, buoyed by renewed trade-deal optimism.

"Easing trade tensions helped lift crude oil prices," ANZ said.

Metals:

Gold prices rose in early trading amid a softer U.S. dollar.

Investors were wary of the limited scope of the U.K. agreement and the upcoming meeting between Washington and Beijing, as negotiations could be long and drawn-out.

"Sentiment remains fragile," Swissquote Bank said.

EMEA HEADLINES

Commerzbank Posts Higher Earnings After Commission Income Lifts Revenue

Commerzbank reported a rise in earnings and revenue for the first quarter that exceeded analysts' expectations, after higher commission income offset a decline in interest income.

The German lender on Friday confirmed its targets for the year, saying it is reducing its reliance on net interest income-the difference between what it earnings from loans and pays for deposits-as interest rates fall. It also reaffirmed its plans for capital returns.

British Airways Owner IAG to Buy 53 Boeing and Airbus Planes After Earnings Rise

British Airways owner International Consolidated Airlines said it ordered 53 planes from Boeing and Airbus to bolster its long-haul fleet after earnings rose in the first quarter of the year.

The airline group, which also houses Iberia and Vueling among others, said it ordered 32 Boeing 787-10 aircraft for British Airways and 21 Airbus A330-900neo aircraft for Aer Lingus, Iberia or Level.

Sonova Shares Jump on Sales Beat, CEO Change

Shares in Sonova rose after the maker of hearing aids posted sales that beat market expectations and said its current chief will be replaced by the former CEO of hearing aids manufacturer WS Audiology.

Shares were up 5.2% at 266.50 Swiss francs in early European trade Friday.

GLOBAL NEWS

China's Exports to U.S. Plunge, in Sign of Bite From Trump Tariffs

China said exports to the U.S. plunged in April, as the Trump administration's tariff assault forced the world's second-largest economy to redirect more of its goods to Southeast Asia, Latin America, Europe and Africa.

Overall, China said its export growth demonstrated surprising resilience last month, with the headline figure showing exports rising 8.1% in dollar-denominated terms in April from a year earlier.

Air-Traffic Control Overhaul Needs Billions in Upfront Investment, Transportation Secretary Says

The Trump administration can revamp the nation's air-traffic control system in three to four years if Congress approves billions of dollars in funding, Transportation Secretary Sean Duffy said Thursday.

The Transportation Department outlined a plan to replace antiquated radar, telecommunications equipment, surveillance systems, air-traffic control towers and other facilities. Duffy didn't share details on the proposed overhaul's cost, but said it would take "lots of billions."

Trump Brings Millionaire Tax Idea Back to Life

WASHINGTON-Fifteen days after taking a marginal tax-rate increase off the table, President Trump put it back in play.

The president, who rejected a "millionaire tax" April 23, is now considering backing a tax structure that would return the top individual income-tax rate to 39.6% from 37% for people making over $2.5 million, according to people familiar with White House discussions.

Write to nina.kienle@wsj.com

Write to us at newsletters@dowjones.com

We offer an enhanced version of this briefing that is optimized for viewing on mobile devices and sent directly to your email inbox. If you would like to sign up, please go to https://newsplus.wsj.com/subscriptions.

This article is a text version of a Wall Street Journal newsletter published earlier today. 




  (END) Dow Jones Newswires



  May 09, 2025 05:04 ET (09:04 GMT)


Copyright (c) 2025 Dow Jones & Company, Inc.

data.title: Pope Leo XIV's election: Pope Francis, like Warren Buffett, mastered the art of succession
data.description:
By Quentin Fottrell

Politicians, corporate leaders and owners of family businesses should take a leaf out of the late pope's playbook

Pope Francis had his eye on the long game.

Just like Warren Buffett, who announced his successor as CEO of Berkshire Hathaway last Saturday, the late pope knew that, in order for his work to continue, he needed to make sure that there were enough like-minded cardinals waiting in the wings who would ensure that the Vatican remained open and accessible in a rapidly changing world.

During his appearance at the Berkshire Hathaway (BRK.B) (BRK.A) annual meeting, investing legend Buffett announced that he intends to step down as CEO at the end of the year, naming longtime Berkshire hand Greg Abel as the man he would like to see fill his shoes. Pope Francis could not name his own successor, but he made sure he had enough lieutenants (or cardinals) in place to ensure there were no unexpected turns.

Pope Francis downplayed the aesthetics of the job to project an 'everyman' appearance of outreach, much like Buffett, who still owns the $31,500 house he purchased at 28.

The Vatican news on Thursday may come as a surprise even to those who devotedly follow Catholic Church politics: Pope Francis elevated the Chicago-born Robert Francis Prevost, now Pope Leo XIV, 69, to cardinal only in 2023. Prevost was considered youthful by College of Cardinals standards and, socially, certainly among the more liberal faction.

While some commentators said the legacy of Pope Benedict XVI was probably his "fashion sense," Pope Francis downplayed the aesthetics of the job to project a more "everyman" appearance of outreach, much like Buffett, who still owns the $31,500 house he purchased at 28 in his hometown of Omaha. No wonder that the current crop of cardinals has been described by many observers as the most diverse ever.

In his first address as pope from the balcony at St. Peter's Basilica in Rome, like his predecessor, Pope Leo XIV spoke of openness: "To the Church of Rome, a special greeting. We must seek together how to be a missionary church, a church that builds bridges, dialogue, always open to receive - like this square with open arms - everyone, all those who need our charity, our presence, dialogue, love."

Warmth, humility, familiarity

It was a speech noted for its warmth and humility. Pope Leo XIV reached out to others beyond the 1.4 billion-strong Roman Catholic Church, in a speech that could have been written for - or by - his predecessor. "This is the peace of the risen Christ, a disarmed peace and a disarming peace, humble and persevering. It comes from God, God who loves us all, unconditionally."

Starting last Wednesday, 133 cardinals were shuttered away in the Sistine Chapel to elect the 267th pope. But Pope Francis, who died on Easter Monday at age 88, was present in spirit. During his papacy, he appointed some 108 cardinal electors, senior cardinals who participate in the Vatican conclave that elects a new pope.

In fact, 80% of the cardinals were appointed by Pope Francis, the singular most important act in solidifying his legacy and making sure that his progressive agenda would continue, most likely to the dismay of the more conservative cardinals in the ranks. Stacking the decks is the cornerstone of any succession plan if the election takes place after you're gone.

Some 80% of the cardinals were appointed by Pope Francis, the singular most important act in solidifying his legacy and making sure that his progressive agenda would continue.

While there are 53 cardinals from Europe, there are 23 from Asia, 18 from Africa and South America, respectively (Pope Francis was Argentinian), and a whopping 20 from North America, plus three from Oceania. Only 22 were appointed by Pope Benedict XVI, who preceded Pope Francis, and even fewer (5) by Pope John Paul II.

During his lifetime, Pope Francis also chose many of the judges for the Court of Cassation, the church's equivalent of the U.S. Supreme Court - it's both the highest venue for appeals and the highest court for interpreting Vatican law. This was not always looked upon favorably by his critics, who said many appointees lacked formal legal training or experience.

Like any successor, whether in a religious organization, a monarchy, corporate America or, indeed, a family business, Pope Leo XIV will have to forge his own path, while maintaining continuity. He has a full plate: maintaining and preserving unity within the church and, like Francis, becoming an emblem of compassion on the international stage.

Centrist, liberal or conservative

Pope Leo appears at present to be something of an enigma. The American Conservative said, "He is considered broadly to be a liberal." In a separate analysis, the BBC said: "He will be seen as a figure who favoured the continuity of Francis' reforms in the Catholic Church. Prevost is believed to have shared Francis' views on migrants, the poor and the environment."

The New Statesman commented: "The election of Pope Leo XIV is a gentle gesture to the importance of moderation, not an ideological handbrake turn (the most radical thing about the appointment is that he is American). After all this handwringing about the battle between liberals and conservatives, it was the centrists at the gate after all."

Francis fostered a culture of inclusivity and humility, while Buffett at Berkshire Hathaway built a culture of trust and accessibility. They both had a strict no-schmucks policy.

"Cardinal Robert Prevost - now Pope Leo XIV - was touted as the man who could do both, who could bridge the ideological gap, who could be a continuity and a change candidate at the same time," the publication added. "His clothes and speech made exactly that case: I look like Benedict, I sound like Francis."

Pope Francis and Warren Buffett created distinct cultures at their respective institutions. They had strict no-schmucks policies. Francis fostered a culture of inclusivity and humility, while Buffett over his six decades at Berkshire Hathaway built a culture of trust and accessibility. Buffett, arguably, had a less "decentralized" approach than Francis did.

Still, it's a hard line to walk. Pope Leo may be the man to walk it. He has both liberal and conservative ideals, not unusual for someone who has risen to the highest ranks of a 2,000-year-old institution, both in relation to his perspectives on theology and social issues. But the lack of clear consensus on where he falls on the left-to-right political spectrum may prove to be one of his strongest assets.

Pope Francis, I imagine, is smiling down.

-Quentin Fottrell

This content was created by MarketWatch, which is operated by Dow Jones & Co. MarketWatch is published independently from Dow Jones Newswires and The Wall Street Journal. 




  (END) Dow Jones Newswires



  05-09-25 0506ET


Copyright (c) 2025 Dow Jones & Company, Inc.

data.title: China to Crack Down on Rare-Earth Materials Ahead of U.S. Trade Talks
data.description:
China has announced a crackdown on the smuggling of critical minerals, coming just one day before trade talks with the U.S., in which rare earth restrictions could be on the table.

Chinese authorities--including ministries of commerce, public security and state security and customs--convened a meeting in the southern port city of Shenzhen on Friday. According to an official readout, the agencies pledged to step up the enforcement of export controls on strategic mineral resources.

Officials at the meeting said that since China implemented export controls on critical minerals such as gallium, germanium, antimony, tungsten and medium and heavy rare earths, some overseas entities have colluded with domestic actors and constantly updated their smuggling methods in an effort to evade enforcement. They did not specify which countries were involved. 




  Write to Singapore Editors at singaporeeditors@dowjones.com 





  (END) Dow Jones Newswires



  May 09, 2025 04:47 ET (08:47 GMT)


Copyright (c) 2025 Dow Jones & Company, Inc.

data.title: That big Boeing plane order? British Airways operator IAG has ordered 32 of its jets.
data.description:
By Barbara Kollmeyer

Boeing shares rose Thursday after U.S. trade official's hint

The parent company of Iberia and British Airways said Friday that it has ordered 32 Boeing jets, confirming hints of such a deal made by Commerce Secretary Howard Lutnick following a trade agreement between the two countries.

Boeing shares (BA) got a boost on Thursday after Lutnick said a U.K. company would buy $10 billion worth of its airplanes.

Read: Trump rolls out U.K. trade agreement. It's a relief - but deals with other countries are more crucial.

International Consolidated Airlines Group (UK:IAG) (ES:IAG), whose shares trade in both the U.K. and Spain, said it ordered the 787-10 aircraft for delivery between 2028 and 2033.

IAG, as it is known, said the Boeing jets are mainly for replacements, and it also ordered 21 Airbus (FR:AIR) A330-900 aircraft.

The airline said the order with Boeing grants British Airways the rights to buy up to 10 additional 787 aircraft and the order with Airbus grants it the right to buy up to 13 additional A330-900neo aircraft.

The airline reported first-quarter operating profit before exceptional items of EUR198 million ($222 million), jumping from EUR68 million ($76 million) a year ago. Revenue rose 9.6% to EUR7 billion.

IAG said it was "continuing to see good demand" for air travel in Latin America and Europe and robust growth in North America, where premium cabin offerings helped compensate for softness in U.S. economy cabin sales. IAG shares rose just over 1% on Friday.

Boeing shares are up just over 8% this year, after a 32% drop in 2024. The aircraft maker last month reported a narrower-than-expected first-quarter loss, citing strength in its commercial airplane business and $545 billion in order backlogs.

The company has estimated tariff hits at less than $500 million a year. Demand for aircraft, mainly Boeing passenger planes, drove a 9.2% surge in durable goods in March as companies sought to stock up ahead of U.S. tariffs.

Qatar Airlines is expected to soon buy 100 Boeing wide-body jets, Bloomberg reported earlier this week, citing sources.

But Boeing's tariff woes could deepen. The European Commission said it is studying retaliation on more than EUR95 billion of U.S. goods if trade talks don't succeed. And the EU was planning to specifically levy Boeing aircraft imports in the event of failed talks, the FT reported earlier this week, citing sources.

-Barbara Kollmeyer

This content was created by MarketWatch, which is operated by Dow Jones & Co. MarketWatch is published independently from Dow Jones Newswires and The Wall Street Journal. 




  (END) Dow Jones Newswires



  05-09-25 0448ET


Copyright (c) 2025 Dow Jones & Company, Inc.

data.title: British Airways Owner IAG to Buy 53 Boeing and Airbus Planes Despite Market Uncertainty — Update
data.description:
By Pierre Bertrand 




  British Airways owner International Consolidated Airlines said it ordered 53 planes from Boeing and Airbus, marking a significant investment in its fleet despite uncertainty in the sector stemming from trade tensions. 



  The airline group, which also houses Iberia and Vueling among others, said it ordered 32 Boeing 787-10 aircraft for British Airways and 21 Airbus A330-900neo aircraft which it said could be deployed to Iberia or its other companies Aer Lingus or Level. 



  The aircraft are due to be delivered between 2028 and 2033, with the orders subject to shareholder approval in June, IAG said Friday. 



  The move comes on the heels of Thursday's framework trade agreement between the U.S. and the U.K., in which both countries agreed to curtail tariffs on certain goods, including Rolls-Royce jet engines and parts. 



  IAG didn't disclose financial details of the order, but Commerce Secretary Howard Lutnick on Thursday said a British airline, which he didn't name, would announce that it is buying $10 billion of Boeing planes. 



  Chief Executive Officer Lluis Gallego, however, stressed in a post-earnings call with journalists on Friday that the plane orders were negotiated ahead of the news of the trade talks. 



  "We welcome the agreement but this negotiation process started a long time ago," Gallego said. 



  As part of the order agreement, British Airways holds the right to purchase up to 10 more U.S.-made Boeing 787s, while IAG can buy up to 13 additional Airbus A330-900neo aircraft, the company said. 



  While U.S. carriers such as American Airlines and Delta Air Lines  have recently withdrawn guidance amid declining domestic demand and tariff-related uncertainty, IAG expressed its confidence in its North Atlantic market. 



  Demand for premium cabin space appears to be  mitigating softness for U.S. point-of-sale economy seating, IAG said after passenger unit revenue for the region increased by 13%, marking the steepest growth across the whole of the company. 



  Strong overall demand for flights meant that its second-quarter was around 80% booked, with revenue ahead of its year-earlier level. 



  IAG maintained its guidance for 2025, although Gallego said it was yet too early to say for sure how the rest of the year will play out 



  In the first three months, IAG swung to a first-quarter profit of 176 million euros, equivalent to $197.6 million, from a loss of 4 million euros in the same period a year earlier. 



  Revenue rose 9.6% to 7.04 billion euros, with growth in both passenger and cargo income. 





  Write to Pierre Bertrand at pierre.bertrand@wsj.com 





  (END) Dow Jones Newswires



  May 09, 2025 04:36 ET (08:36 GMT)


Copyright (c) 2025 Dow Jones & Company, Inc.

data.title: Panasonic Holdings to Cut 10,000 Jobs Globally — Update
data.description:
By Kosaku Narioka 




  Panasonic Holdings plans to cut about 10,000 jobs globally as it reviews operational efficiency at each group company. 



  The Japanese electronics maker said Friday that it would re-evaluate the numbers of organizations and personnel needed, and terminate loss-making businesses that have no prospect of improving profit. 



  Panasonic said the measures would be carried out mainly in the fiscal year that started in April and target 5,000 employees in Japan and 5,000 employees overseas. 



  As of the end of September, Panasonic had about 229,000 employees worldwide. 



  The Japanese company said it expects restructuring costs of 130 billion yen, equivalent to $891 million, for this fiscal year. 



  Panasonic said it aims to improve profit by at least Y150 billion for the fiscal year starting in April 2026 through restructuring, compared with the year that ended March. 





  Write to Kosaku Narioka at kosaku.narioka@wsj.com 





  (END) Dow Jones Newswires



  May 09, 2025 04:44 ET (08:44 GMT)


Copyright (c) 2025 Dow Jones & Company, Inc.

data.title: News Highlights: Top Energy News of the Day - Friday at 4 AM ET
data.description:
Oil Futures Rally on U.S.-U.K. Trade Deal Optimism

Oil futures rallied on news of a trade deal between the U.S. and the U.K. that includes lowering the tariff on British-made cars imported into the U.S. and exempting some goods from an overall 10% tariff. 

 
U.S. Natural Gas Futures Slip as Storage Builds

Natural gas futures lost a few cents in choppy trade as a weekly storage report confirmed an expected increase in the surplus over the five-year average. 

 
Conoco  Cuts CapEx View on Volatile Economic Status

The Houston oil producer posted first-quarter earnings of $2.8 billion, or $2.23 a share, up from $2.6 billion, or $2.15 a share, a year earlier. 

 
Siemens Energy Expects Limited Hit From U.S. Tariffs

The maker of energy equipment reported a surge in profit and anticipates limited direct impact from U.S. tariffs in its fiscal second half. 

 
Energy & Utilities Roundup: Market Talk

Find insight on Ithaca Energy, crude futures, Centrica and more in the latest Market Talks covering Energy and Utilities. 

 
Trump Says He Hasn't Decided if Iran Can Enrich Uranium in a New Deal

The president's latest comments add to the administration's mixed messages as it pursues talks for a new nuclear pact with Tehran. 

 
Syria Dangles Oil, Peace for U.S. Help on Sanctions

The former Islamist rebels who run the new government are looking to convince a skeptical Trump administration 

 
U.S. Crude Oil Stockpiles Fall Amid Lower Production

U.S. crude oil inventories fell by 2 million barrels to 438.4 million barrels in the week ended May 2 and were about 7% below the five-year average for the time of year, the EIA said. 

 
Orsted Warns of Earnings Hit After Axing U.K. Offshore Wind Project

The Danish company won't continue the Hornsea 4 offshore wind project in the U.K. due to rising costs. 

 
Veolia to Buy CDPQ's Stake in Water Technologies and Solutions for $1.75 Billion

The deal means Veolia takes full ownership of Caisse de depot et placement du Quebec's Water Technologies and Solutions subsidiary.

(END) Dow Jones Newswires

May 09, 2025 04:15 ET (08:15 GMT)

Copyright (c) 2025 Dow Jones & Company, Inc.
